Metadata.title: Gangan & Partners — Law Firm
Metadata.description: Gangan & Partners law firm (Kyiv) — defense in criminal proceedings, transnational investigations, crypto fraud, AML and cybercrime cases.

Practices.title: Areas of Law
Practices.sub: Specialized legal assistance in the most complex and technologically demanding areas of law.

list.title: Criminal Defense
list.desc: Defending suspects and defendants at every stage of criminal proceedings — from pre-trial investigation to appeal.
intro: Gangan & Partners provides full-cycle defense in criminal proceedings — from the moment of detention or summons for questioning through to a final, legally binding verdict.
Our team has significant experience in cases involving economic crimes, corruption, abuse of office, and organized crime.
services: Defense in courts of first instance
Acquittals and case closures on rehabilitating grounds

list.title: Transnational Investigations
list.desc: Cases with an international element: extradition, mutual legal assistance, investigation of financial crimes.
intro: The international nature of financial crimes requires a lawyer who understands not only Ukrainian law, but also the mechanisms of international cooperation: MLAT, Interpol, EU directives.
services: Coordination with foreign counsel and jurisdictions

list.title: Crypto Fraud & Blockchain
list.desc: Recovering assets frozen on exchanges. Investigating DeFi fraud, rug pulls and NFT abuse.
intro: Crypto fraud is one of the fastest-growing categories of crime. Our team combines legal and technical expertise to effectively protect your interests.
services: Liaising with crypto exchanges to freeze assets
Defense in rug pull and NFT abuse cases

list.title: Money Laundering / AML
list.desc: Defense in money laundering cases. Compliance audits, liaison with FATF and regulators.
intro: AML proceedings in Ukraine are becoming increasingly complex. We provide defense for individuals and businesses in cases initiated by NABU, the State Financial Monitoring Service, and foreign regulators.
services: Defense in criminal proceedings under Art. 209 of the Criminal Code of Ukraine

list.title: Cybercrime
list.desc: Defense in cases of unauthorized access, hacking, phishing and cyber investigations.
intro: Cybercrime covers a broad range of conduct — from unauthorized access to computer systems to large-scale attacks on critical infrastructure.
services: Defense in cases under Art. 361-363 of the Criminal Code of Ukraine

list.title: Blockchain Investigations
list.desc: Tracing on-chain transactions, identifying wallets, building an evidentiary record from on-chain data.
intro: On-chain analysis allows fund movements to be traced even through mixers and anonymous wallets. We use leading analytics platforms to build the evidentiary record.
services: Liaison with Chainalysis and other platforms

AI.sub: Our AI assistant provides an initial legal orientation for your situation. For a detailed case analysis, book a paid consultation with a lawyer.

Blog.title: Analysis & Commentary
Blog.sub: Legal analysis, case-law reviews and commentary from the Gangan & Partners team.
Blog.noPosts: No articles in this category yet.

fallbackPosts.title: New FATF Requirements for Crypto Businesses: What Changed in 2025
fallbackPosts.excerpt: An analysis of the latest FATF recommendations and their impact on Ukrainian crypto-service providers.
body: In 2025, FATF updated its recommendations for virtual asset service providers (VASPs). The changes affect customer identification procedures, the Travel Rule, and reporting to regulators.
For Ukrainian crypto companies, this means tighter compliance requirements and a need to review internal AML/CFT policies.
Gangan & Partners supports businesses in adapting to the new requirements — from auditing internal procedures to representation in dealings with regulators.

fallbackPosts.title: DeFi Fraud: Mechanisms for Recovering Funds Through Litigation
fallbackPosts.excerpt: A practical overview of legal protection tools for victims of decentralized finance protocols.
body: The growing popularity of DeFi protocols has been accompanied by a rise in fraud schemes — rug pulls, fake liquidity pools, phishing smart contracts.
Unlike traditional fraud, on-chain analytics plays a key role in DeFi cases: transaction tracing makes it possible to identify perpetrators' wallets even after funds are withdrawn through mixers.
In this article we cover a step-by-step action plan for victims — from preserving evidence to filing a report with the Cyber Police and engaging exchanges to freeze assets.

fallbackPosts.title: Art. 209 of the Criminal Code: Defense Practice in Money Laundering Cases
fallbackPosts.excerpt: A review of key Supreme Court rulings and defense strategies in 2024–2025.
body: Article 209 of the Criminal Code of Ukraine (legalization of criminally obtained income) remains one of the most difficult provisions to qualify.
In this article we analyze key Supreme Court rulings from the past two years and practical defense strategies that have proven effective in our clients' cases.

fallbackPosts.title: Phishing Attacks on Business: What to Do in the First 48 Hours
fallbackPosts.excerpt: An action plan for companies after discovering a phishing attack or corporate email compromise.
body: The first 48 hours after a cyber incident is discovered determine whether you'll be able to preserve evidence and minimize losses.
We cover a step-by-step plan: isolating compromised systems, preserving evidence, and notifying the Cyber Police and partner banks.

News.title: Firm News
News.sub: Recent developments, cases and recognitions of Gangan & Partners.
News.noNews: No news in this category yet.

fallbackNews.title: Gangan & Partners Launches Dedicated Crypto Fraud Practice
fallbackNews.excerpt: In response to growing demand, the team has spun off crypto fraud and blockchain investigations into a dedicated practice area.
body: Gangan & Partners announces the launch of a dedicated practice focused on investigating crypto fraud and tracing blockchain transactions.
The decision follows a significant increase in inquiries from individuals and businesses affected by DeFi fraud, rug pulls, and frozen exchange accounts.
The new practice will be led by a team combining legal expertise with technical skills in on-chain data analysis.

fallbackNews.title: Client Regains Access to Assets Frozen by a Crypto Exchange
fallbackNews.excerpt: The Gangan & Partners team secured the unblocking of a client's account after three months of negotiations with an international exchange.
body: After three months of negotiations and evidence preparation, the Gangan & Partners team secured the unblocking of a client's crypto account, which had been frozen by an international exchange over suspected links to a fraud scheme.
The case required simultaneous work with the exchange's compliance department and preparation of documentation confirming the legitimate origin of the funds.
Case details are withheld at the client's request.

fallbackNews.title: Gangan & Partners Recognized as an Emerging Firm in Blockchain Law
fallbackNews.excerpt: An industry publication recognized the team for its distinctive approach to investigations at the intersection of criminal law and blockchain analytics.
body: Gangan & Partners received recognition from an industry publication covering the Ukrainian legal market, in the category of emerging teams in the law of new technologies.
The jury's commentary highlighted the firm's unique combination of classic criminal practice with technical expertise in blockchain transaction analysis.

fallbackNews.title: New Partner Joins the Blockchain Investigations Practice
fallbackNews.excerpt: Experience in tracing crypto transactions and building evidentiary records for court will strengthen the firm's crypto practice.
body: Gangan & Partners is pleased to announce a new partner specializing in blockchain investigations and the preparation of analytical reports for court proceedings.
This addition reflects the firm's strategy of growing technical expertise alongside its classic criminal practice.

About.title: About the Firm
About.sub: Gangan & Partners is a next-generation law firm: we advise crypto and fintech businesses, defend clients in criminal proceedings, and lead transnational investigations — where the law is still catching up with technology.
history: Gangan & Partners was founded by a team of lawyers who recognized the growing need for defense in cases at the intersection of criminal law and emerging technology.
Today we are a team of attorneys, analysts and technical specialists working on cases that combine classic criminal law with modern challenges: cryptocurrency, blockchain, cybercrime.
About.mission: To provide the highest level of legal defense where the stakes are highest, and where case complexity demands both legal and technical expertise.

Privacy.title: Privacy Policy
Privacy.placeholderNote: This is a template privacy policy describing the site's technical structure. It should be reviewed and approved by a data-protection lawyer before publication.

sections.heading: Data we collect
sections.body: When you fill out a contact form, subscribe to our newsletter, or chat with the AI assistant, we receive the data you provide: name, contact details, and message content. We do not collect data automatically without your explicit action, aside from basic visit analytics.

sections.heading: Cookies
sections.body: The site may use cookies for traffic analytics and to remember your preferences (e.g. selected language). You can decline non-essential cookies via the banner at the bottom of the page.

sections.heading: Third-party services
sections.body: We use: Sanity.io (blog content storage), Anthropic (processing AI assistant requests — messages you send in the chat are sent to Anthropic to generate a response), Vercel (hosting), and, if configured, Formspree (contact form and newsletter processing).

sections.heading: Your rights
sections.body: You have the right to request access, correction, or deletion of your personal data. Contact us at gangan.partners@gmail.com.

FAQ.title: Frequently Asked Questions
FAQ.sub: General answers to common questions about criminal procedure, crypto assets, and cybercrime in Ukraine.
FAQ.disclaimer: This information is for general education only and does not replace legal advice about your specific situation.
FAQ.moreQuestions: Didn't find an answer to your question?

list.q: What are your rights immediately after detention or being notified of suspicion?
list.a: Under Articles 42 and 208 of the Criminal Procedure Code (CPC) of Ukraine, you have the right to know the reason for detention, to remain silent, to a defense lawyer from the moment of detention (including a free state-appointed one), to notify relatives, and to challenge the detention before an investigating judge. Warrantless detention cannot exceed 72 hours before a court must rule on a preventive measure.

list.q: What happens during the pre-trial investigation?
list.a: Investigation begins once information is entered into the Unified Register of Pre-Trial Investigations. General deadlines run up to 12 months for minor/moderate offenses and 18 months for serious/especially serious ones before a suspicion notice is served; shorter deadlines apply afterward. Exact timelines depend on the offense classification and case specifics.

list.q: Can a crypto exchange freeze or seize your assets?
list.a: Yes — Article 170 of the CPC allows seizure of "virtual assets" within criminal proceedings; investigators typically request the exchange to freeze the account. You can challenge the seizure before an investigating judge, and challenge any transfer to the Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA) during confiscation proceedings. Whether virtual assets qualify as "material evidence" remains legally contested in Ukrainian courts.

list.q: What does Article 209 of the Criminal Code (money laundering) cover?
list.a: Article 209 criminalizes acquiring, possessing, using, or disposing of property known to derive from criminal activity, including financial transactions or concealment of its origin. Enhanced penalties apply for "large" (over 6,000 tax-free minimum incomes) and "especially large" (over 18,000) amounts. The article was substantially amended by Law No. 361-IX, effective April 28, 2020.

list.q: What should a cyberattack or phishing victim do?
list.a: Report to the Cyber Police via ticket.cyberpolice.gov.ua, email callcenter@cyberpolice.gov.ua, phone 0 800 50 51 70 (weekdays, 09:00–18:00), or in person at any police station. Preserve evidence beforehand — screenshots, correspondence, transaction records. Filing opens a case number in the Unified Register of Pre-Trial Investigations.

list.q: What's the difference between "suspicion" and a formal "charge"?
list.a: A person becomes a "suspect" upon receiving a written notice of suspicion or upon detention — during the investigation stage. A person becomes formally "accused" only once the prosecutor files an indictment with the court, setting out the established facts and legal classification. Suspicion marks the investigation stage; formal charge marks the transition to trial.

list.q: How does extradition to/from Ukraine work, at a basic level?
list.a: The Prosecutor General's Office handles extradition requests during investigation or trial; the Ministry of Justice handles cases involving convicted persons. Requests proceed under applicable treaties or diplomatically on a reciprocity basis where none exists. A simplified procedure is available with the person's consent and an investigating judge's approval.

list.q: Is cryptocurrency legal in Ukraine as of 2026?
list.a: Owning and trading cryptocurrency isn't criminalized, but a dedicated regulatory framework is still incomplete. The 2022 "On Virtual Assets" law was signed but never took effect, pending Tax Code amendments. A new bill (No. 10225-d) passed its first reading in September 2025 and remains pending — treat this area as evolving, not settled.

WalletScreen.title: Crypto Wallet Screening
WalletScreen.sub: Preliminary check of a wallet address against US OFAC sanctions lists. Free, no signup.
WalletScreen.resultInvalid: Address format not recognized. Bitcoin and Ethereum are supported.
WalletScreen.resultFlaggedTitle: Match found on a sanctions list
WalletScreen.resultFlaggedNote: This address appears on the OFAC SDN List as of {date}. Before taking any action, consult a lawyer for a full review.
WalletScreen.resultClearNote: This address was not found in the checked OFAC list as of {date}. This does NOT confirm the absence of risk — it was checked against a single public sanctions list only, without transaction history analysis, mixer-link detection, or other lists (EU, UK, UN).
WalletScreen.disclaimer: This tool is a preliminary indicator, not a professional AML/KYC screening or legal opinion. The list is built from public OFAC data and updated manually, so it may lag behind the official registry. Use a licensed screening service for compliance purposes.

Notary.title: Blockchain Document Notarization
Notary.sub: Anchor proof of a document's existence into the Bitcoin blockchain — free, no signup, the file itself is never uploaded.
Notary.dropHint: Choose a file — hashing happens in your browser, the file itself is never sent anywhere.
Notary.resultDone: Proof file (.ots) downloaded. Keep it together with the original document — you'll need it for verification.
Notary.resultError: Couldn't create the proof. Please try again later.
Notary.howItWorks: How it works: your browser computes the file's SHA-256 hash, and only that hash (not the file) is sent to the public calendar servers of the OpenTimestamps protocol, which anchor it into the Bitcoin blockchain. The resulting .ots file is a standard, open format you can independently verify at opentimestamps.org.
Notary.disclaimer: Full blockchain confirmation (inclusion in a block) usually takes a few hours. The .ots file you receive immediately records a preliminary timestamp; for full independent verification, use opentimestamps.org later. This does not substitute for a legal opinion on a document's evidentiary weight in a specific case.

Confidential.title: Confidential Inquiry
Confidential.sub: For especially sensitive matters — your message is encrypted in your browser and can only be read by the firm.
Confidential.encryptedBadge: End-to-end encrypted — even we can't see the content until we decrypt it with our own private key.
Confidential.contactPlaceholder: Email, Telegram, or any channel you consider safe
Confidential.error: Failed to send. Please try again.
Confidential.notConfigured: This form isn't connected to a service yet — add NEXT_PUBLIC_FORM_ENDPOINT in .env.local.
Confidential.successBody: Your encrypted message has been received. We'll contact you via the channel you provided.
Confidential.howItWorks: How it works: your message is encrypted in your browser (RSA + AES-256) with the firm's public key before it's sent. Only whoever holds the matching private key can decrypt it.

Decrypt.title: Decrypt a Message
Decrypt.sub: Internal tool. The private key and content never leave this browser.
Decrypt.decryptError: Couldn't decrypt. Check the key and the message.
Decrypt.note: Decryption happens entirely on your device — nothing is sent anywhere.
